bull_thesis:
ALTEN is the largest pure-play European engineering and technology consulting firm, and it is trading at roughly six times price-to-free-cash-flow on a fortress balance sheet (net cash of about EUR 59 million; cash of EUR 348 million exceeds debt of EUR 289 million). That valuation reflects a rough five years: a multi-year, multi-sector collapse in R&D budgets compressed full-year 2025 net income to EUR 106.9 million, down 42.6% year over year, with earnings per share falling to EUR 3.03 — the lowest in five years and roughly a quarter of the 2022 peak of EUR 458 million.

The bull case is that this is a cyclical trough being mispriced as something more permanent. Three things support that reading. First, the first quarter of 2026 delivered the first positive organic revenue growth in over a year — up 1.6% at constant perimeter (the reported figure was negative 0.8%, dragged down by a stronger euro on the roughly 65% of revenue earned outside France). The verticals growing are exactly the ones tied to long-cycle European capital-spending programmes: aerospace up 2%, defence and security up 10%, energy and nuclear up 10%, rail returning to growth. The declining segments — automotive down 2.6%, semiconductors down 8%, telecoms down 18% — are the cyclically depressed legacy book. Second, cash conversion is genuinely intact. Free cash flow (FCF) was EUR 239.9 million, a 7.8% FCF margin, funding four self-financed acquisitions and a maintained EUR 1.50 dividend with no added leverage, alongside a live buyback of up to 7% of share capital for cancellation, announced in May 2026 and executing through June. Third, return on invested capital (ROIC, the profit the business earns on the money tied up in it) of roughly 10.6% and return on equity of 12.8% remain above the cost of capital even at this trough, which is the hallmark of a quality cyclical rather than a balance-sheet trap.

Founder-led, conservative governance rounds the picture: management's approach of 'margin improves gradually' is the same under-promise-and-deliver-cash pattern that held operating margin on activity at roughly 8.5% through the downturn via selling and administrative cost discipline.

On normalized through-cycle net income of roughly EUR 180–200 million — between the 2024 figure of EUR 186 million and a partial recovery — at a quality-cyclical multiple of 11–13 times plus net cash, intrinsic value is materially above the roughly EUR 63 live price. The screener's discounted-cash-flow, earnings power value, and owner-earnings frameworks produce margins of safety of 59%, 39%, and 48% respectively, all pointing the same way. For this thesis to pay off, the aerospace, defence, and nuclear cycle needs to scale fast enough to offset the declines in automotive, semiconductors, and telecoms; the euro needs to stabilize; and the offshoring and AI pressure on engineering hours needs to prove cyclical rather than a permanent bill-rate erosion.
bear_thesis:
The cheapness is partly deserved, and most of the discount is shared sector exposure rather than a single-name edge. ALTEN's gross margin is only about 8.3% and shrinking — a structurally low-value-add, labour-arbitrage delivery model versus true-intellectual-property peers such as Cognizant (34% gross margin), EPAM, and Globant (27–35% gross margin), and even versus French peer Sopra Steria (roughly 17% gross margin). The entire European IT and engineering-consulting cohort has repriced on the same artificial-intelligence and labour-deflation concern: Capgemini now trades at roughly 7.4 times forward price-to-earnings, down from low-double-digits before these fears took hold. So ALTEN at roughly six times price-to-free-cash-flow is cheap within a already-repriced peer group — buying the gap mostly buys exposure to the same sector theme on an unresolved structural question, not a company-specific advantage.

The earnings base has stepped down through five consecutive halvings from the peak: net income EUR 458 million (2022) to EUR 233 million to EUR 186 million to EUR 107 million (2025); earnings per share from EUR 13.21 to EUR 3.03; a three-year compound annual growth rate of negative 38.8%. The recovery is unproven and back-half-loaded: management itself guides first-half 2026 organic revenue to zero to negative 0.5% and declines to give a dated margin target. The most telling unguarded comment from the first-quarter 2026 earnings call was on semiconductors — management conceded that offshoring 'is a real topic that has to be discussed,' meaning part of the demand softness may be a permanent relocation of billable hours, not a cycle.

ALTEN's economics are built on billing engineering and R&D labour-hours at a spread — precisely the model that generative AI threatens to deflate through hour-decoupling and bill-rate pressure. Demand weakness is broad: automotive, semiconductors, telecoms, life sciences, Germany, the Nordics, Eastern Europe, and North America are all negative. The most recent quarterly earnings per share were 81% below the prior year; the Piotroski score (a financial-health composite) is only 4 out of 9. What would confirm the bear case: a second flat-to-negative organic print in the second half of 2026, continued gross-margin contraction, or evidence that the semiconductor and German-automotive weakness is structural — at which point the apparent 'trough' re-anchors lower and the optical six-times multiple becomes a melting-base trap.
consensus_delta:
For a deep-value name the key question is whether the market is pricing terminal decline into a company with stable earnings power, or pricing a genuine recovery into an eroding base. Here the market is not clearly wrong — and the current investing environment cuts against the name.

The screener flags consensus forward earnings-per-share growth of 12.9% and a discounted-cash-flow margin of safety of 59%, but both rest on assumptions that are now stale: the DCF extrapolates peak free cash flow (the EUR 141.8 fair-value figure is an outlier), and peer multiples are anchored to pre-correction levels rather than today's. Marked to current prices, Capgemini is at roughly 7.4 times forward price-to-earnings and the entire European engineering-consulting cohort has re-rated together on AI and labour-arbitrage fear — so ALTEN's roughly six-times price-to-free-cash-flow is cheap within a repriced group. The gap is shared sector exposure, not a company-specific mispricing.

The genuinely contrarian reading is narrow: that the 2025 net income of EUR 107 million is a cyclical trough (aerospace, defence, and nuclear capital spending pulls it back toward EUR 180–200 million) rather than a structurally lower base. But management's own first-half 2026 organic guide of zero to negative 0.5% and the unresolved semiconductor-offshoring admission mean even the optimistic case concedes the inflection is marginal and back-half-loaded. The market is roughly right here, not wrong-footed.
forcing_function:
There is no hard, contractual catalyst that would shatter consensus expectations. The nearest dated events carry low asymmetry: the 2025 dividend ex-date of 22 June 2026 and payment of 24 June 2026 represent a known, already-priced cash return — effectively spent as a re-rating trigger; the annual general meeting was 18 June 2026; and first-half 2026 results (expected late August or September 2026) are the next real data point but are pre-guided to flat-to-down organic revenue, so they are more likely to confirm the soft patch than to force a re-rating higher.

The buyback-for-cancellation is supportive but gradual and non-binary — it is not an event. The true re-rating driver is a slow, undated fundamental one: multiple consecutive quarters of accelerating organic growth as the aerospace, defence, and nuclear cycle scales and automotive and semiconductor declines stabilize. That is precisely the kind of 'wait for the fundamentals' catalyst that scores as a dated but soft catalyst with no contractual payout — not the hard, contractual catalyst that commands a premium. There is no proxy fight, no strategic review, no announced asset sale, no activist on the register.
valley_of_death:
Over the next three to nine months the balance sheet removes any existential risk — net cash of roughly EUR 59 million, free cash flow of EUR 239.9 million, no debt maturity wall, no cash-burn pressure, and an Altman-Z score (a composite financial-distress gauge) of 4.0. The danger is to the investment narrative, not to solvency. First, first-half 2026 organic revenue is guided at zero to negative 0.5%, so the next one or two reports will likely show flat-to-down revenue and keep earnings per share depressed — the most recent quarter was 81% below the prior year — giving the market no trigger to re-rate. Second, a stronger euro continues to drag the roughly 65% of revenue earned internationally, widening the gap between reported and organic figures that management relies on in its communications. Third, German automotive R&D (down roughly 30%) and semiconductors (down 8%, with the offshoring overhang) could deteriorate further before aerospace, defence, and nuclear scale enough to offset them, pushing the apparent trough lower. Fourth, telecoms down 18% and life-sciences weakness show the soft patch is broad-based.

None of these kill the company, but each one delays the re-rating and risks a lower earnings anchor — the classic deep-value time-decay where you are right on the balance sheet and wrong on timing.
positioning_washout: There is no mechanical forced-selling setup here. ALTEN is not a high-yield or heavily passive-owned name whose investors would be forced to sell on a dividend cut — the EUR 1.50 dividend was maintained, not cut, so there is no yield-driven forced selling. The company is founder-influenced with a stable shareholder register; the live buyback-for-cancellation (up to 7% of share capital) is steady price support, the opposite of a selling wave. There is no identifiable forced-seller dynamic — no index deletion, no covenant breach, no distressed holder — to create the kind of capitulation flush that a 'wait for the wash-out' verdict would require. The stock simply drifts with European IT-services sentiment.
moderator_conclusion:
Pass for now, conviction 2 out of 5. ALTEN is a genuinely good business at a fair-to-cheap price, but it is not an asymmetric special situation, and the current investing environment actively argues against it.

Three things converge on a pass. First, when peer multiples are marked to today they erase most of the apparent discount. Capgemini at roughly 7.4 times forward price-to-earnings and a cohort-wide re-rating mean ALTEN's roughly six-times price-to-free-cash-flow is cheap within a repriced group; the gap is shared sector exposure on the unresolved AI and labour-arbitrage driver, not a company-specific edge. On that basis the sum-of-the-parts (valuing the operating business separately from the net-cash balance) fair value of roughly EUR 58 sits right at the EUR 57.6 scan price and slightly below the roughly EUR 63 live price.

Second, the catalyst is a dated but soft one with no contractual payout at best. The only dated events — the dividend and the annual general meeting — have already fired and been priced; the real driver is an undated, multi-quarter fundamental recovery that management itself guides flat-to-down for the first half of 2026.

Third, the current regime (the portfolio's tactical stance as of June 2026, which favours hard-dated structural special situations in thinly-covered, distressed, spinoff, or forced-seller situations) explicitly penalises theses that hinge on one shared macro or secular factor. ALTEN is a well-covered large-cap whose thesis turns on a single shared question — whether engineering labour-arbitrage is structurally impaired by AI and offshoring — exactly the kind of resolution-dependent thesis the regime says to deprioritise.

The net-cash balance sheet and EUR 240 million of free cash flow mean there is no capital-destruction risk, which is why this is a measured pass and not a short.

Separately, the value case judged on its own merits (setting aside the catalyst and regime tilt) scores a 3 out of 5: the valuation is fair-to-modestly-cheap and the forensics are clean — real cash, disciplined acquisitions, maintained dividend. But the eroding moat (gross margin roughly 8.3% and contracting, ROIC stepped down from the peak) and the meaningful labour-arbitrage secular tail cap the value conviction at 3. A low multiple on a base that may be structurally, not just cyclically, lighter is fair value, not a bargain. If the second half of 2026 prints show accelerating organic growth and gross-margin stabilisation, this graduates to a watchlist entry pending a better entry point; until then, pass.
sop_breakdown: The base case reconciles the EUR 76 bull scenario and the EUR 41 bear scenario toward an operating-earnings anchor, weighted for the meaningful (though not terminal) secular tail and the back-half-loaded recovery. For the operating business: normalized through-cycle net income of roughly EUR 160 million — the 2025 trough of EUR 107 million recovering only partway, haircut for gross-margin erosion and AI and offshoring drag — at roughly 11.5 times earnings. That multiple reflects a de-rated quality cyclical: a premium to the roughly 7.4-times cohort for the net-cash balance sheet and ROIC above cost of capital, but a discount to true-intellectual-property peers because of the 8% gross margin. That produces roughly EUR 1.84 billion, or roughly EUR 54 per share. Adding net cash of EUR 59 million (roughly EUR 1.70 per share) and the per-share tailwind from the up-to-7%-of-capital buyback for cancellation (roughly EUR 1–2 per share) puts the base sum-of-the-parts fair value at roughly EUR 57–58 per share — essentially in line with the roughly EUR 63 live price and modestly above the EUR 57.6 scan snapshot. The screener's discounted-cash-flow fair value of EUR 141.8 is an outlier that extrapolates peak free cash flow and is set aside; the earnings-power-value anchors at roughly EUR 44 sit below this base, so EUR 58 is a balanced midpoint.
risk_reward: Roughly symmetric, not asymmetric. From the roughly EUR 63 live price: the upside to the base fair value of roughly EUR 58 is actually slightly negative — the re-rated cohort multiple no longer preserves the headline margin of safety the scan showed; the upside to the EUR 76 bull scenario is roughly 21%; the downside to the EUR 41 bear scenario is roughly 35%. From the EUR 57.6 scan snapshot: upside to fair value is roughly flat, upside to the bull scenario is 32%, downside to the bear is 29%. The net picture is a roughly one-to-one risk-to-reward ratio, mildly skewed toward the downside at the live price. The net-cash balance sheet floors the downside, but the eroding gross margin and the secular tail cap the upside. This is not the convex deep-value payoff that a raw six-times price-to-free-cash-flow headline implies once peer multiples are marked to today.
peer_comps_note:
The peer file's verdict of 'cheap versus peers' is directionally right but needs to be updated to today's repriced cohort. ALTEN's true comparables are European and global IT and engineering-consulting firms: Sopra Steria, Capgemini, CGI, Cognizant, EPAM, and Globant. Capgemini now trades at roughly 7.4 times forward price-to-earnings (enterprise value divided by earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation of roughly 7.4 times) as of mid-June 2026 — the entire cohort has compressed on the same AI and labour-arbitrage-deflation fear, so this is a sector-wide re-rating driven by a shared structural concern, not a company-specific mispricing. ALTEN and its peers move together on the same unresolved question.

ALTEN's roughly 8.3% gross margin is far below Cognizant (34%), EPAM and Globant (27–35%), and even Sopra Steria (roughly 17%), confirming a genuinely lower-value-add, more labour-intensive model — so the discount is partly deserved. Anchoring to the live roughly 7–8 times cohort price-to-earnings (not the pre-correction low-double-digit memory), ALTEN's net-cash balance sheet and ROIC above cost of capital justify a modest premium of roughly 11 times on normalized earnings, which is roughly where the EUR 58 base sum-of-the-parts lands. The apparent six-times price-to-free-cash-flow discount shrinks materially once peers are marked to current prices; do not credit the gap as a company-specific edge.
interrogator_dossier:
# ALTEN SA (ATE.PA) — Forensic Dossier

Engineering and Technology Consulting / IT services. Euronext Paris. Price roughly EUR 63 (scan snapshot EUR 57.6). Financial year ends 31 December.

Source note: No earnings-call transcript was bundled with the screener data. This dossier is built from the live first-quarter 2026 earnings call (held roughly mid-May 2026, via Investing.com transcript), the full-year 2025 annual results (released roughly late February or early March 2026), the 2026 annual general meeting and dividend notice, and the screener's financial trajectory block. The peer set — Sopra Steria, CGI, Cognizant, EPAM, Globant, Capgemini, and Akkodis/Adecco — is used as the relative lens. ALTEN is a single-business engineering R&D outsourcer, not a conglomerate, so there is no segment-revenue breakdown; the sum-of-the-parts is a whole-company intrinsic value framed by end-market and geographic mix.

## 1. Narrative Arc

ALTEN's story has inverted over five years. The 2021–2022 narrative was a post-Covid super-cycle: revenue ran from EUR 2.93 billion (2021) to EUR 3.78 billion (2022, up 29%) with net income exploding to EUR 458 million and earnings per share of EUR 13.21 — a once-in-a-decade demand snap-back as automotive, aerospace, and technology R&D budgets all reopened at once, amplified by a low-tax and one-off-flattered base. Management's tone was offensive: hiring tens of thousands of engineers, 'we cannot recruit fast enough,' international expansion.

By 2023–2024 the narrative quietly shifted to normalisation: revenue plateaued at roughly EUR 4.07–4.14 billion, but net income collapsed from the EUR 458 million peak to EUR 233 million (2023) then EUR 186 million (2024) as the gross-margin and utilisation tailwind reversed. The 2022 earnings per share of EUR 13.21 was never a run-rate — it was a peak-margin print, and management never re-anchored investors away from it.

By full-year 2025 the narrative turned overtly defensive: revenue actually fell to EUR 4,099 million (down 1.1% reported, down 4.5% organically), net income more than halved again to EUR 106.9 million (down 42.6% year over year), and the entire investor message pivoted to 'margin resilience and cash generation in a soft cycle.' Operating margin on activity held at roughly 8.5% through selling and administrative cost cuts and project management discipline; free cash flow of EUR 239.9 million funded four self-financed acquisitions and a maintained EUR 1.50 dividend. The growth drivers emphasised in 2021–2022 — automotive R&D, telecoms, semiconductors — are now the problem children; the new leading verticals are aerospace, defence and security, energy and nuclear, and rail.

In first-quarter 2026 the arc continues: headline revenue EUR 1.7 billion (down 0.8% reported), but management leads with 'we have managed to come back to an increasing organic turnover by 1.6%' at constant perimeter — i.e. the story is now 'the foreign-exchange and perimeter headline understates an organic inflection.' First-half 2026 organic revenue is guided at zero to negative 0.5%, so even the optimistic framing concedes the first half is flat-to-down; the recovery is a second-half 2026 and 2027 story.

Net arc: from 'structural compounder riding an engineering super-cycle' to 'quality cyclical defending margin and cash through a multi-sector R&D-budget recession, waiting on aerospace, defence, and nuclear to pull it out.'

## 2. Claims Versus Financials

Management claim (first-quarter 2026 or full-year 2025): 'Back to increasing organic turnover, plus 1.6%.' What the financials show: full-year 2025 organic was negative 4.5%; reported revenue was negative 1.1% year over year; first-quarter 2026 reported was negative 0.8%. Assessment: partly true, heavily framed. A positive 1.6% organic at constant perimeter is real but is flattered by acquisitions in the perimeter and masked by a foreign-exchange drag; the reported line is still negative and the first-half guide is zero to negative 0.5%. The inflection is marginal, not a trend break.

Management claim: 'Margin resilience — operating margin on activity roughly 8.5%.' What the financials show: net margin of 2.6% (2025) versus roughly 6% in 2022; net income down 42.6%. Assessment: divergent. 'Operating profit on activity' is held up by cost cuts, but the bottom line fell sharply — net income of EUR 107 million is the lowest in five years and roughly a quarter of the 2022 peak of EUR 458 million. Margin 'resilience' is being achieved by cost defence, not pricing power; gross margin is 8.3% and contracting.

Management claim: 'Strong cash generation, self-financed four acquisitions, net cash.' What the financials show: free cash flow of EUR 239.9 million for full-year 2025 (7.8% FCF margin); net cash roughly EUR 59 million; cash of EUR 348 million exceeds debt of EUR 289 million. Assessment: corroborated. This is the most credible part of the story — ALTEN genuinely converts earnings to cash and funds acquisitions and a dividend without leverage. Net cash is a real addition to intrinsic value.

Management claim: 'Recovery led by aerospace, defence, and nuclear — first-quarter 2026 aerospace plus 2%, defence plus 10%, energy plus 10%.' What the financials show: these are small relative to the weak book; automotive at 13% of group revenue is down 2.6%, semiconductors down 8%, telecoms down 18%, life sciences declining. Assessment: a mix-shift hope, not yet a number. The growing verticals are real but not yet large enough to offset the declining ones — hence first-half 2026 organic revenue at roughly zero.

Management claim (implied by screener): 'Consensus forward earnings per share growth of 12.9%; discounted-cash-flow margin of safety 59%.' What the financials show: earnings-per-share trajectory of EUR 13.21 (2022) to EUR 6.74 to EUR 5.33 to EUR 3.03 (2025), a three-year compound annual growth rate of negative 38.8%. The forward earnings-per-share figures cited in the screener do not reconcile with EUR 106.9 million net income divided by roughly 34 million shares, which implies roughly EUR 3.10 per share. Assessment: forward optimism built on a deeply depressed base. The street models a recovery off EUR 107 million; treat EUR 3.03 as the real 2025 anchor and treat higher figures as foreign-exchange or data-aggregation artefacts.

Management claim: 'Itsclinic deal — EUR 60 million revenue, 470 consultants, operating margin improvable to 10% quickly; close expected third-quarter 2026.' What the financials show: a bolt-on acquisition of roughly 1.5% of group revenue. Assessment: credible and consistent with the disciplined, self-funded acquisition engine; not a needle-mover.

Synthesis: the cash claims are corroborated; the organic-inflection and margin-resilience claims are real but heavily framed and not yet visible in the bottom line, which is at a five-year low. The narrative is modestly more optimistic than the trajectory justifies on growth, and honestly below the 2022 peak that some investors still anchor to.

## 3. Tone and Confidence Trajectory

Trajectory: declining (from offensive to defensive), with a tentative first-quarter 2026 stabilisation.

2021–2022 (inferred from trajectory): offensive — record hiring, double-digit growth, expansion. Confidence high. Full-year 2025 call: defensive — the message is cost discipline, cash, and 'visibility remains uncertain.' Language shifted to efficiency and balance-sheet quality. First-quarter 2026: cautiously stabilising but hedged. Management leads with the positive 1.6% organic but the dominant texture is uncertainty: 'It is extremely difficult to have a forecast.' 'Macroeconomic, geopolitical context is of a high uncertainty.' On semiconductors: 'It is a real topic that has to be discussed.' On margin they refuse to commit to a number: 'The increase of margin is going to happen by and by.' That is the language of a team that wants to signal a bottom without being held to a timeline.

The shift from 'we can't hire fast enough' (2022) to 'the increase of margin is going to happen by and by' (2026) is the single clearest tone tell: from demand-constrained growth to cost-managed defence.

## 4. Guidance Credibility

Reliability: medium.

ALTEN has historically been a conservative French mid-cap that under-promises on margin and delivers cash — and full-year 2025 did deliver on cash (EUR 239.9 million free cash flow, dividend maintained at EUR 1.50, net cash). On that axis credibility is intact. But on trajectory, the company has repeatedly framed each soft year as near a bottom while net income kept halving (EUR 458 million to EUR 233 million to EUR 186 million to EUR 107 million). The screener records a beat rate of only 50% (one quarter out of two) against a collapsing earnings base, which is not a strong delivery record on growth. Management is now explicitly declining to give a hard margin target ('by and by') and has guided first-half 2026 organic to soft zero to negative 0.5%, which is appropriately humble. Summary: credible on cash and balance sheet; weak-to-unproven on the timing of the earnings recovery.

## 5. Analyst Pressure Points

Automotive and Germany. Automotive is 13% of group revenue and German automotive R&D is described as down roughly 30%; analysts press on when this stops bleeding. Management has no firm answer — 'extremely difficult to forecast.'

Semiconductors and offshore shift. Semiconductors are down 8% and management flags a structural question about work shifting offshore — analysts are probing whether this is cyclical or a permanent erosion of onshore demand and bill rates. Management does not resolve it.

Margin cadence. Analysts want a dated margin path; management deflects to 'by and by,' the classic non-answer.

Telecoms down 18%. A 'very strong decrease' that gets acknowledged but is given no recovery timeline.

AI and automation of engineering hours. Analysts increasingly ask whether generative AI compresses billable R&D hours. Management's answer is a mix-shift toward 'complex' work — a redirect, not a refutation.

## 6. Red Flags and Green Flags

Red flags. Net income has fallen through five consecutive halvings of the 2022 peak: EUR 458 million to EUR 107 million (2025), earnings per share from EUR 13.21 to EUR 3.03; a negative 38.8% three-year compound annual growth rate. The earnings base is genuinely shrinking, not just pausing. Gross margin of only roughly 8.3% and contracting confirms a low-value-add, labour-intensive delivery mix with little pricing power relative to peers. Demand weakness is broad, not isolated: automotive, semiconductors, telecoms, life sciences, Germany, the Nordics, Eastern Europe, and North America are all negative — a multi-vertical R&D-budget recession. The most recent quarterly earnings per share were 81% below the prior year, and the Piotroski score is only 4 out of 9. The semiconductor-offshoring admission leaves the key structural question unresolved.

Green flags. Genuine net cash (roughly EUR 59 million; cash EUR 348 million exceeds debt EUR 289 million) and free cash flow of EUR 239.9 million — a real fortress balance sheet and a real addition to intrinsic value; this is not a distressed company. Organic revenue returned to positive 1.6% at constant perimeter in first-quarter 2026 — the first positive organic in over a year, a tentative bottoming signal. Aerospace up 2%, defence and security up 10%, energy and nuclear up 10%, and rail returning to growth — the European defence and nuclear capital-spending cycle is a real, multi-year tailwind that ALTEN is genuinely exposed to. The self-funded acquisition engine (Itsclinic plus 'two deals practically finished, five underway, ten in the wings') is disciplined and accretive, with no leverage. Founder-led, conservative governance; dividend maintained at EUR 1.50 despite the earnings trough. Return on invested capital of 10.6% and return on equity of 12.8% remain above cost of capital even at the trough — a quality cyclical, not a value trap.

## 7. Hidden Signals

The 'by and by' margin language is a deliberate hedge — management believes margin recovers but will not be pinned to a quarter, consistent with a team that has been early in calling the bottom each year before. The vertical rotation in the commentary — aerospace, defence, nuclear, and rail now lead the script; automotive, telecoms, and semiconductors have moved from headline drivers to problem children — mirrors the same playbook as the broader European engineering-consulting cohort rotating toward defence. The foreign-exchange drag is now a recurring theme: the international book (65% of revenue) is being dinged by a stronger euro; management leans on this to explain the reported-versus-organic gap, which is legitimate but also convenient. The 'offshore is a real topic' admission on semiconductors is the most important unguarded line — it concedes that part of the demand softness may be a permanent relocation of work rather than a cycle. The dividend was held flat rather than raised, signalling that management itself views this as a trough year and not yet a base to grow from.

## 8. Capital-Allocation Verdict

Classification: stable on capital allocation; mildly deteriorating on earnings power. ALTEN is not deteriorating financially — it is a net-cash, cash-generative, disciplined-acquirer operator with a maintained dividend. But earnings power has structurally stepped down (net income EUR 458 million to EUR 107 million) and the recovery is unproven and back-half-loaded.

The single most important thing to weigh before sizing: is the 2025 earnings trough cyclical — R&D budgets reopen as the aerospace, defence, and nuclear cycle scales, and ALTEN re-rates off a EUR 107 million base toward EUR 200 million or more — or is part of it a structural bill-rate and hour erosion from offshoring and generative-AI automation of engineering hours? The valuation (roughly six times price-to-free-cash-flow, net cash, positive discounted-cash-flow and earnings-power-value margins of safety) prices in the bear case; the question is purely whether the earnings base is melting or merely depressed.

Economic moat: narrow. Moat type is a blend of switching cost and intangible relationship — embedded multi-year engineering teams inside clients such as Airbus, Stellantis, and Thales, security clearances for defence work, and domain expertise — plus scale advantage as the largest pure-play European engineering-consulting firm with roughly 52,000 engineers. But the 8.3% gross margin and ROIC stepping down from the peak to roughly 10.6% show that this is a narrow moat with limited pricing power: a high-quality labour-arbitrage business, not an intellectual-property monopoly. ROIC still exceeds the cost of capital, so the moat is real but thin.

Moat trend: eroding mildly. Gross margin is contracting, return on invested capital and net margin have fallen materially from the 2022 peak, and the semiconductor-offshoring admission points to bill-rate pressure. The moat is not collapsing — the business is still cash-generative and organic growth has returned to positive — but the direction is down, not up.

Secular threat: meaningful, not yet terminal. ALTEN sits squarely in the AI-automation-of-engineering and IT-services-labour-arbitrage concern: its economics are billing R&D and engineering labour-hours at a spread, exactly the model generative AI threatens to deflate through book-to-bill pressure, hour decoupling, and offshoring. However, unlike a pure code body-shop, a large portion of ALTEN's work is regulated, safety-critical, and security-cleared engineering — aerospace certification, nuclear, defence — that is far harder to automate or offshore. That is why the threat is meaningful rather than terminal, and why the defence and nuclear rotation matters as a structural hedge.

Cheapness verdict: mostly a fear-driven price on a business trading at a partial discount it has partly earned. Six times price-to-free-cash-flow on a net-cash, EUR 240 million free-cash-flow, return-on-invested-capital-above-cost-of-capital business that has merely cycled down is cheap — but the roughly 8% gross margin and the live offshoring and AI question mean the discount to higher-quality peers (Cognizant 34% gross margin, EPAM and Globant 27–35% gross margin) is partly justified. This is cheap-but-deservedly-cheaper, with a real cyclical recovery option and a real but manageable secular tail.

Management credibility 3 of 5; trajectory turning from deteriorating toward tentative stabilisation; moat narrow but real; moat trend eroding mildly; secular threat meaningful.
